items.title: IgA vasculitis
items.contentHtml:
IgA vasculitis in children

  • Henoch-Schönlein Purpura is a systemic leukocytoclastic vasculitis mediated by deposits of immunoglobulin A in small vessels, predominating in skin, gastrointestinal tract, joints and kidney.
  • The diagnosis is clinical; The skin biopsy shows leukocytoclastic vasculitis with positive immunofluorescence for immunoglobulin A.
  • In children, it is the most common vasculitis, with peak incidence between 2 and 10 years, and a slight predominance in males (ratio 1.5:1).
  • The most common trigger is an upper respiratory tract infection due to group beta-hemolytic streptococcus. Aor other pathogens (viruses, Mycoplasma).

items.title: Palpable purple
items.contentHtml:
Palpable purpura in the lower extremities

  • Palpable purpura (non-thrombocytopenic) is the mandatory diagnostic criterion according to the classification of the American College of Rheumatology (1990) and the EULAR/PRINTO/PRES criteria (2008).
  • It is typically located in the lower extremities and buttocks, due to the effect of gravity; it can ascend to the trunk and arms.
  • The lesions are erythematous macules that evolve into papules and petechiae, they do not disappear under vitro pressure.
  • Key differential diagnosis: immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) – here the platelet count is normal (>150,000/μL); If there is thrombocytopenia, rule out ITP or coagulopathies.

items.title: Non-erosive arthritis
items.contentHtml:
Non-erosive arthritis/arthralgias

  • The arthritis in Henoch-Schönlein Purpura is oligoarticular, symmetrical and non-erosive, affecting mainly knees and ankles.
  • It occurs in 60-85% of cases, often as an initial symptom, and can precede purpura.
  • Arthritis is self-limited (resolves in days to weeks) and does not leave deformities or radiological sequelae.
  • Treatment is symptomatic with rest and paracetamol (dose: 10-15 mg/kg/dose every 6 hours); Avoid non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs due to the risk of worsening intestinal and renal vasculitis.

items.title: Colicky abdominal pain
items.contentHtml:
Recurrent colic abdominal pain

  • Colic abdominal pain occurs in 50-70% of children with Henoch-Schönlein Purpura, secondary to vasculitis of the intestinal submucosa.
  • It can simulate a surgical abdomen; abdominal ultrasound shows thickening of the intestinal wall (intestinal target sign or target pattern).
  • The presence of severe and persistent abdominal pain is an indication for corticosteroids (prednisone 1-2 mg/kg/day orally) to reduce edema and prevent intussusception or perforation.
  • Differential diagnosis: acute appendicitis, intestinal intussusception, inflammatory disease intestinal; The absence of high fever and marked leukocytosis indicates Henoch-Schönlein Purpura.

items.title: Hematochezia
items.contentHtml:
Hematochezia due to intestinal vasculitis

  • Hematochezia (red blood in stool) is a manifestation of severe intestinal vasculitis, present in 20-30% of cases.
  • It may be accompanied by melena or occult blood; endoscopy reveals petechiae, ulcers and erythema in the gastric and duodenal mucosa.
  • Massive bleeding is rare but potentially fatal; requires hospitalization, intestinal rest and intravenous corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 1-2 mg/kg/day).
  • If there are signs of intestinal ischemia or perforation, exploratory laparotomy is indicated.

items.title: IgA nephritis
items.contentHtml:
IgA nephritis as a complication

  • Immunoglobulin A nephritis is the most serious complication, it occurs in 20-50% of cases, generally in the first 4-6 weeks of the onset of purpura.
  • It manifests with microscopic or macroscopic hematuria, proteinuria (< 1 g/m²/day in mild forms) and arterial hypertension.
  • Renal biopsy is indicated if there is persistent proteinuria > 1 g/day or nephrotic syndrome; shows mesangial deposits of immunoglobulin A.
  • Treatment of severe nephritis includes corticosteroids (prednisone 2 mg/kg/day for 4 weeks) plus angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors (enalapril 0.1-0.5 mg/kg/day) for proteinuria.

items.title: Scrotal edema
items.contentHtml:
Scrotal edema in boys

  • Acute, painful or painless scrotal edema is an atypical but specific manifestation in prepubertal boys (2-15% of cases), due to vasculitis of the scrotal vessels.
  • It can be confused with testicular torsion; Doppler ultrasound shows increased blood flow and thickening of the tunica vaginalis, ruling out torsion.
  • Treatment is conservative (rest, elevated scrotum); Systemic corticosteroids only if there is severe pain or association with abdominal pain.
  • The differential diagnosis also includes epididymitis and incarcerated inguinal hernia.

items.title: Infectious trigger
items.contentHtml:
Frequent infectious trigger

  • In up to 50% of cases, Henoch-Schönlein Purpura is preceded by an upper respiratory tract infection, mainly due to group A beta-hemolytic streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes).
  • Other associated agents: Epstein-Barr virus, Parvovirus B19, Mycoplasma pneumoniae, Campylobacter jejuni and Haemophilus influenzae type b.
  • Streptococcal pharyngitis is confirmed with throat culture or rapid antigen test; Antibiotic treatment (oral penicillin V 25-50 mg/kg/day for 10 days) does not prevent vasculitis but treats the infection.
  • Triggers have also been described by vaccines (MMR, hepatitis B) and drugs (antibiotics, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs).

items.title: typical age
items.contentHtml:
Typical age between 2 and 10 years

  • Henoch-Schönlein Purpura is the most common vasculitis in childhood, with peak incidence between 2 and 10 years (average 6 years).
  • It is rare in children under 2 years of age and in adolescents; in adults it is usually more severe, with greater kidney involvement.
  • The annual incidence is estimated at 10-20 cases per 100,000 children, with a predominance in autumn and winter.
  • In children under 2 years of age, atypical presentation (purpura limited to the buttocks, without abdominal pain) can delay the diagnosis.

items.title: Corticosteroids in abdominal pain
items.contentHtml:
Corticosteroids in severe abdominal pain

  • Systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 1-2 mg/kg/day orally, maximum 60 mg/day) are indicated in severe abdominal pain, significant hematochezia or recurrent intestinal intussusception.
  • The duration of treatment is 2-4 weeks, with gradual reduction; the response is usually rapid (24-48 hours).
  • They are not routinely recommended for cutaneous purpura or arthritis, since they do not shorten the duration of the rash or prevent nephritis.
  • Adverse effects to monitor: high blood pressure, hyperglycemia, osteoporosis; in children, growth retardation with prolonged use.

items.title: Kidney prognosis
items.contentHtml:
Long-term renal prognosis

  • The renal prognosis depends on the severity of the initial nephritis: nephrotic proteinuria (> 3.5 g/day) and persistent hematuria are associated with a higher risk of chronic kidney disease.
  • In children, the majority (80-90%) recover completely; 5-10% develop chronic kidney disease and less than 1% progress to end-stage renal disease.
  • Poor prognostic factors are: age > 10 years, persistent proteinuria > 1 g/day for more than 6 months, and arterial hypertension at the beginning.
  • Follow-up with blood pressure control, proteinuria in morning urine and serum creatinine should be for at least 5 years, even if the initial nephritis was mild.

items.foco: Avoid NSAIDs due to kidney risk
items.title: Avoid NSAIDs
items.contentHtml:
Avoid NSAIDs due to renal risk

  • Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (ibuprofen, naproxen, diclofenac) are contraindicated in Henoch-Schönlein Purpura due to their nephrotoxic potential and risk of worsening intestinal vasculitis.
  • NSAIDs inhibit cyclooxygenase, reducing the synthesis of renal prostaglandins, which can precipitate acute renal failure in a kidney already compromised by immunoglobulin A deposits.
  • For the management of joint or abdominal pain, paracetamol is preferred (10-15 mg/kg/dose every 6-8 hours) or, if necessary, mild opioids such as tramadol (1-2 mg/kg/dose every 6 hours).
  • In case of severe arthritis that does not respond to paracetamol, prednisone can be considered at low doses (0.5-1 mg/kg/day) for the short term, avoiding NSAIDs.
